subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! Look around you for a moment. If you live near the Jubba or Shabelle rivers, you may see fields of green maize and tall sorghum swaying in the wind. If you live in Hargeisa, Mogadishu, or a village in the countryside, you have surely seen a mango tree heavy with fruit, a banana plant with its broad leaves, or a farmer scattering bean seeds into the soil just as the rainy season begins. Every one of those plants started as a tiny seed, no bigger than your fingernail. In this unit, you will discover the amazing secret of how that happens.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 5 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name and describe the three main parts of a seed: the embryo, the food store, and the seed coat.
Explain germination and put its stages in the correct order, from the dry seed to the first green leaves.
List the conditions a seed needs to germinate (water, warmth, and air) and explain why light is NOT needed for germination.
Describe what a growing plant needs to stay healthy: light, water, nutrients from the soil, space, and a suitable temperature.
Plan and carry out a fair test about plant growth, changing only one thing at a time and keeping everything else the same.
Measure and record plant growth carefully using a ruler and a results table, and draw a simple growth graph.
Describe the life cycle of a flowering plant: seed, germination, growth, flower, fruit, and new seeds.
Explain asexual reproduction and give local examples: banana suckers, sweet-potato and cassava cuttings, and onion bulbs.
Connect what you learn to real farming and gardening along the rivers and across the dry lands of East Africa.
Here are the important words for this unit. Do not try to memorise them all in one day. Learn a few each day, say them out loud, and look for them as you read the lesson.

concepts.title: The Secret Inside a Seed
concepts.explanation: Let us begin our journey where every plant begins: inside a seed. Hold a dry bean in your hand. It feels hard, still, and lifeless. But this is one of the most wonderful things in all of creation, because sleeping inside that little bean is a complete baby plant, packed with everything it needs to begin life. A seed is like a tiny lunchbox with a sleeping traveller inside and enough food for the start of the journey. Every seed, whether it is a giant mango stone or a tiny sesame seed, has the same three main…
concepts.example: The seed coat is the tough outer skin. It is the guard of the seed. It protects the delicate parts inside from being crushed, from drying out, and from insects. When you soak a bean in water overnight, the seed coat…

concepts.title: How a Seed Wakes Up (Germination)
concepts.explanation: Germination is the word scientists use for the moment a resting seed wakes up and starts to grow. Think of a farmer near Kismaayo who plants sorghum just before the rains. For days the seeds lie hidden and still in the dry soil. They are dormant, alive but resting. Then the rain falls, the soil becomes moist and warm, and something amazing happens. The seeds begin to grow. That waking-up is germination. Germination always happens in the same order. Learning this order is one of your main goals for this unit, so…
concepts.example: The seed drinks water. The dry seed soaks up water from the moist soil and swells. This water wakes up the living parts inside and softens the seed coat.

concepts.title: What a Seed Needs to Germinate
concepts.explanation: Not every seed that falls to the ground will grow. A seed needs the right conditions first. Through careful experiments, scientists (and farmers!) have discovered that a seed needs three things to germinate: water, warmth, and air. Let us look at each one. Water is the most important. Water wakes the seed, softens the coat, and starts the chemical changes that break down the food store into energy the embryo can use. A seed in bone-dry soil stays dormant. This is why farmers along the Jubba and Shabelle wait for…
concepts.example: Warmth speeds germination up. Seeds sprout faster in warm soil and slowly (or not at all) in cold soil. The warm East African climate is good for germinating many crops quickly, which is one reason maize, cowpeas, and…

concepts.title: What a Growing Plant Needs
concepts.explanation: Once a seed has germinated and its green leaves have opened, the young plant is no longer living on its packed lunch. It must now feed itself and keep growing. A growing plant, just like a growing child, has needs. If those needs are met, the plant grows strong. If they are not, it grows weak, turns yellow, or dies. Healthy plants need light, water, nutrients, space, and a suitable temperature. Green plants make their own food. This is one of the most important ideas in all of biology. In a process called…
concepts.example: Water does two big jobs. It is a raw material for photosynthesis, and it also carries dissolved nutrients from the roots all the way up to the leaves, like a delivery system running through the whole plant. Nutrients…

concepts.title: Thinking Like a Scientist
concepts.explanation: Everything we have learned so far was discovered by people asking questions and testing them carefully. You can do the same. When a scientist wants to find out what affects plant growth, they run a fair test. This is one of the most important skills in this whole unit, so let us learn it well. A fair test is one where you change only one thing and keep everything else the same. Suppose Hassan wants to find out whether warmth helps beans germinate faster. He sets up two cups of beans. In one, he changes the warmth…
concepts.example: Scientists use three helpful words. The thing you deliberately change is the independent variable. The thing you measure to see the result is the dependent variable (for example, how many seeds sprout, or how tall the…

concepts.title: The Life Cycle of a Flowering Plant
concepts.explanation: So far we have followed a plant from seed to seedling. But the story does not end there. A plant grows up, makes flowers, and produces new seeds of its own, which can grow into the next generation. This never-ending circle is called the life cycle. Let us walk around the circle of a flowering plant, such as a bean or a sunflower. Seed. The cycle begins (we can say) with a seed, resting and ready.
concepts.example: Germination. With water, warmth, and air, the seed germinates and a seedling appears.

explorations.title: Looking Inside a Seed
explorations.context: To find and identify the three main parts of a seed: the seed coat, the food store, and the embryo. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Before you start, write down what you think you will find inside a bean seed. What do you expect the baby plant to look like?…
explorations.prompt: Which part was the largest? Why do you think the food store is so big?
explorations.answer: Which part was the largest? Why do you think the food store is so big? Why is the seed coat tough and on the outside? The maize grain did not split into two neat halves like the bean. What does this tell you about different kinds of seeds?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find and identify the three main parts of a seed: the seed coat, the food store, and the embryo. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Before you start, write down what you think you will find inside a bean seed. What do you expect the baby plant to look like?…

explorations.title: The Germination Jar
explorations.context: To watch the stages of germination happen with your own eyes, and to see the root grow down and the shoot grow up. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write down which you think will appear first from the seed: the root or the shoot. I predict that... will appear…
explorations.prompt: Which appeared first, the root or the shoot? Was your prediction correct?
explorations.answer: Which appeared first, the root or the shoot? Was your prediction correct? Which direction did the root grow? Which direction did the shoot grow? On which day did you first see a green colour, and why did green appear only after the shoot was up?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch the stages of germination happen with your own eyes, and to see the root grow down and the shoot grow up. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write down which you think will appear first from the seed: the root or the shoot. I predict that... will appear…

explorations.title: What Does a Seed Need? (A Fair Test)
explorations.context: To find out whether seeds need water to germinate, by running a fair test. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seeds with ... will germinate, and the seeds with ... will not, because...
explorations.prompt: In which cup did the most seeds germinate? Why?
explorations.answer: In which cup did the most seeds germinate? Why? Why did the dry cup (A) fail to germinate? The flooded cup (C) often fails too. Explain why, using what you learned about seeds needing air. What does this fair test prove that a seed needs?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether seeds need water to germinate, by running a fair test. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seeds with ... will germinate, and the seeds with ... will not, because...

explorations.title: Does a Plant Need Light? (A Fair Test)
explorations.context: To find out whether a growing seedling needs light to stay healthy, by comparing plants in light and in the dark. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seedling in the light will..., and the seedling in the dark will..., because...
explorations.prompt: Which plant stayed green and strong? Which turned pale and weak?
explorations.answer: Which plant stayed green and strong? Which turned pale and weak? Plants make their own food using light in a process called photosynthesis. Use this to explain your result. Why was it important to give both plants the same amount of water?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether a growing seedling needs light to stay healthy, by comparing plants in light and in the dark. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seedling in the light will..., and the seedling in the dark will..., because...

methods.title: Looking Inside a Seed
methods.example: To find and identify the three main parts of a seed: the seed coat, the food store, and the embryo. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Before you start, write down what you think you will find inside a bean seed. What do you expect the baby plant to look like?…
steps: The evening before, put 4 or 5 beans in the bowl of water and leave them to soak overnight.
The next day, take out the soaked beans. Notice how they have swelled up and softened.
Wash your hands. Gently peel off the soft outer skin. This is the seed coat.
Carefully split the bean into its two halves. These thick halves are the food store (cotyledons).
Look between the halves for a tiny curved shape with a little point. This is the embryo, the baby plant. Use the magnifying glass to see the tiny root and shoot.

methods.title: The Germination Jar
methods.example: To watch the stages of germination happen with your own eyes, and to see the root grow down and the shoot grow up. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write down which you think will appear first from the seed: the root or the shoot. I predict that... will appear…
steps: Line the inside of the jar with a thick layer of damp cotton wool or crumpled tissue.
Slide the beans down the side so they are pressed between the glass and the cotton, where you can see them. Space them out.
Add a little water to keep the cotton moist but not flooded. There should be no pool of water at the bottom, because the seeds still need air.
Put the jar in a warm place. It does NOT need to be in the light.
Each day, check the jar. Add a few drops of water if the cotton is drying out. Do not let it flood.

methods.title: What Does a Seed Need? (A Fair Test)
methods.example: To find out whether seeds need water to germinate, by running a fair test. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seeds with ... will germinate, and the seeds with ... will not, because...
steps: Put cotton wool and 3 beans in each of the three cups, exactly the same in every cup.
Cup A (dry): add no water at all. Keep it in a warm place.
Cup B (moist): add a little water to dampen the cotton. Keep it in the same warm place.
Cup C (flooded): fill it with water so the beans are completely under water. Keep it in the same warm place.
Keep everything else the same: same beans, same cups, same warm spot, same amount of air. Only the water is different.

methods.title: Does a Plant Need Light? (A Fair Test)
methods.example: To find out whether a growing seedling needs light to stay healthy, by comparing plants in light and in the dark. Make a Prediction (Hypothesis) Write: I predict that the seedling in the light will..., and the seedling in the dark will..., because...
steps: Choose two seedlings that are about the same height and health.
Put one on a sunny windowsill (the light plant).
Put the other in a dark cupboard or under a box (the dark plant).
Give both plants the SAME small amount of water on the same days. Keep them in rooms of similar warmth.
The only difference is light. Everything else is kept the same.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 1
workedExamples.prompt: Which part of a seed is the tiny baby plant? a) seed coat b) food store c) embryo d) flower
workedExamples.solution: 1 c) embryo The embryo is the tiny baby plant; the other parts protect or feed it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: What is the main job of the seed coat? a) to make food b) to protect the inside c) to attract bees d) to grow roots
workedExamples.solution: 2 b) to protect the inside The tough seed coat guards the embryo and food store.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these does a seed NOT need to germinate? a) water b) warmth c) air d) light
workedExamples.solution: 3 d) light Seeds use their own food store, so they germinate even in the dark. Light matters only after leaves appear.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: During germination, which part usually appears first? a) the flower b) the root c) the fruit d) the first leaves
workedExamples.solution: 4 b) the root The root (radicle) grows down first to anchor the plant and take in water.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A seed that is alive but resting and not growing is said to be: a) dead b) dormant c) ripe d) dispersed
workedExamples.solution: 5 b) dormant A dormant seed is alive but resting, waiting for the right conditions.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Green plants make their own food by a process called: a) germination b) pollination c) photosynthesis d) dispersal
workedExamples.solution: 6 c) photosynthesis Photosynthesis uses sunlight to make sugar (food) in the leaves.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a drought-resistant crop grown in dry areas of East Africa? a) banana b) sorghum c) mango d) tomato
workedExamples.solution: 7 b) sorghum Sorghum survives with little water, so it suits dry East African areas.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A new banana plant is usually grown from: a) a seed b) a sucker c) a flower d) pollen
workedExamples.solution: 8 b) a sucker Bananas are grown asexually from suckers, not from seeds.

practice.answer: 1 c) embryo The embryo is the tiny baby plant; the other parts protect or feed it.

practice.prompt: A new banana plant is usually grown from: a) a seed b) a sucker c) a flower d) pollen
practice.answer: 8 b) a sucker Bananas are grown asexually from suckers, not from seeds.

practice.prompt: In a fair test on plant growth, you should change: a) nothing b) everything c) only one thing d) two things
practice.answer: 9 c) only one thing Changing one thing at a time is what makes a test fair.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why do farmers space out their maize plants? a) to look neat b) so plants do not compete for light and water c) to use more land d) to attract birds
practice.answer: 10 b) so plants do not compete for light and water Crowded plants fight for resources and grow poorly.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The food store inside a bean seed is made of two halves called: a) cotyledons b) petals c) roots d) sepals
practice.answer: 11 a) cotyledons The two food-store halves of a bean are the cotyledons.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which method spreads seeds using the wind? a) a coconut floating at sea b) a fluffy seed carried on the breeze c) a bird eating a berry d) a bean growing from a sucker
practice.answer: 12 b) a fluffy seed carried on the breeze Fluffy or winged seeds are spread by wind.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Looking Inside a Seed
activities.materials: A few large dry beans (such as kidney beans or broad beans) and a few maize grains; A small bowl of clean water; A saucer or plate; A magnifying glass if you have one (not essential); Paper and a pencil or coloured pencils for drawing
steps: The evening before, put 4 or 5 beans in the bowl of water and leave them to soak overnight.
The next day, take out the soaked beans. Notice how they have swelled up and softened.
Wash your hands. Gently peel off the soft outer skin. This is the seed coat.
Carefully split the bean into its two halves. These thick halves are the food store (cotyledons).
Look between the halves for a tiny curved shape with a little point. This is the embryo, the baby plant. Use the magnifying glass to see the tiny root and shoot.

activities.title: The Germination Jar
activities.materials: A clean clear glass jar or a clear plastic cup; Cotton wool, or tissue paper, or a paper towel; 3 or 4 beans or maize grains; Clean water; A ruler and your notebook
steps: Line the inside of the jar with a thick layer of damp cotton wool or crumpled tissue.
Slide the beans down the side so they are pressed between the glass and the cotton, where you can see them. Space them out.
Add a little water to keep the cotton moist but not flooded. There should be no pool of water at the bottom, because the seeds still need air.
Put the jar in a warm place. It does NOT need to be in the light.
Each day, check the jar. Add a few drops of water if the cotton is drying out. Do not let it flood.

activities.title: What Does a Seed Need? (A Fair Test)
activities.materials: 3 small clear cups or jars, labelled A, B, and C; Cotton wool or tissue; 9 beans (3 in each cup); Clean water
steps: Put cotton wool and 3 beans in each of the three cups, exactly the same in every cup.
Cup A (dry): add no water at all. Keep it in a warm place.
Cup B (moist): add a little water to dampen the cotton. Keep it in the same warm place.
Cup C (flooded): fill it with water so the beans are completely under water. Keep it in the same warm place.
Keep everything else the same: same beans, same cups, same warm spot, same amount of air. Only the water is different.

activities.title: Does a Plant Need Light? (A Fair Test)
activities.materials: 2 healthy young bean seedlings grown in cups (from Experiment 2 or newly grown), as alike as possible; A dark cupboard or a box that blocks light; A sunny windowsill; Water and a ruler
steps: Choose two seedlings that are about the same height and health.
Put one on a sunny windowsill (the light plant).
Put the other in a dark cupboard or under a box (the dark plant).
Give both plants the SAME small amount of water on the same days. Keep them in rooms of similar warmth.
The only difference is light. Everything else is kept the same.

activities.title: Observe: Thinking Like a Scientist
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of thinking like a scientist around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: The Life Cycle of a Flowering Plant
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the life cycle of a flowering plant around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Seed
A structure holding a baby plant and stored food in a protective coat.
Embryo
The tiny baby plant inside a seed.
Food store (cotyledon)
The part of a seed that feeds the embryo before it can make its own food.
Seed coat
The tough outer skin that protects the seed.
Germination
The process where a seed begins to grow into a new plant.
Dormant
Alive but resting, waiting for the right conditions to grow.
Root
Grows down; anchors the plant and takes in water and nutrients.
Shoot
Grows up; becomes the stem and leaves.
Nutrients
Mineral foods from the soil that a plant uses to build new parts.
Photosynthesis
How green leaves use sunlight to make food from water and air.
Life cycle
The repeating circle of stages from seed back to seed.
Pollination
Moving pollen so that seeds can form.

vocabulary.meaning: A structure holding a baby plant and stored food in a protective coat.
vocabulary.example: Every one of those plants started as a tiny seed, no bigger than your fingernail.

vocabulary.meaning: The tiny baby plant inside a seed.
vocabulary.example: When you split a soaked bean, the little plant shape you see is the embryo.

vocabulary.meaning: The part of a seed that feeds the embryo before it can make its own food.
vocabulary.example: The two thick halves of a bean seed are its food store.

vocabulary.meaning: The tough outer skin that protects the seed.
vocabulary.example: The skin you peel off a soaked bean is the seed coat.

vocabulary.meaning: The process where a seed begins to grow into a new plant.
vocabulary.example: Over the next few weeks you will learn how a seed is built, how it wakes up and begins to grow in a process called germination, what a plant needs to grow strong and healthy, and how plants make new plants.

vocabulary.meaning: Alive but resting, waiting for the right conditions to grow.
vocabulary.example: Bean seeds stay dormant in a dry store until they are planted and watered.

vocabulary.meaning: Grows down; anchors the plant and takes in water and nutrients.
vocabulary.example: It is small, but if you look carefully at a soaked bean you can see a tiny root and a tiny shoot already formed, waiting.

vocabulary.meaning: Grows up; becomes the stem and leaves.
vocabulary.example: The first green shoot pushes up out of the soil toward the sunlight.

vocabulary.meaning: Mineral foods from the soil that a plant uses to build new parts.
vocabulary.example: The part of a plant that grows down to anchor it and take in water and nutrients.

vocabulary.meaning: How green leaves use sunlight to make food from water and air.
vocabulary.example: A mango tree's leaves carry out photosynthesis all day long.

vocabulary.meaning: The repeating circle of stages from seed back to seed.
vocabulary.example: A bean plant's life cycle takes about three months from planting to new seeds.

vocabulary.meaning: Moving pollen so that seeds can form.
vocabulary.example: After pollination, part of the flower grows into a fruit that holds new seeds inside.

commonMistakes: Seeds use their own food store in the dark soil.
The seed is only resting, not dead.
Plants eat food from the soil.
Soil gives water and minerals, not ready-made food.
Too much water removes air and rots the seed.
Many grow from suckers, bulbs, tubers, or cuttings.

fluency.prompt: Why do farmers space out their maize plants? a) to look neat b) so plants do not compete for light and water c) to use more land d) to attract birds
fluency.answer: 10 b) so plants do not compete for light and water Crowded plants fight for resources and grow poorly.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 b) so plants do not compete for light and water Crowded plants fight for resources and grow poorly.

fluency.prompt: The food store inside a bean seed is made of two halves called: a) cotyledons b) petals c) roots d) sepals
fluency.answer: 11 a) cotyledons The two food-store halves of a bean are the cotyledons.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 a) cotyledons The two food-store halves of a bean are the cotyledons.

fluency.prompt: Which method spreads seeds using the wind? a) a coconut floating at sea b) a fluffy seed carried on the breeze c) a bird eating a berry d) a bean growing from a sucker
fluency.answer: 12 b) a fluffy seed carried on the breeze Fluffy or winged seeds are spread by wind.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 b) a fluffy seed carried on the breeze Fluffy or winged seeds are spread by wind.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to solve these real problems.
realProblems.answer: The seeds did not get water, so they stayed dormant and could not germinate. Farah should water the soil (or wait for rain, or irrigate) so the seeds have the moisture they need.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The seeds did not get water, so they stayed dormant and could not germinate. Farah should water the soil (or wait for rain, or irrigate) so the seeds have the moisture they need.

realProblems.prompt: Farah plants bean seeds in dry soil and no rain comes for two weeks. The seeds do not sprout. Explain why, and say what Farah should do.
realProblems.answer: The wet corner is waterlogged, so there is no air for the seeds and they rot. The farmer should improve drainage, plant on raised ridges, or use that corner for a crop that tolerates wet ground.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The wet corner is waterlogged, so there is no air for the seeds and they rot. The farmer should improve drainage, plant on raised ridges, or use that corner for a crop that tolerates wet ground.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River has a low, wet corner of the field where seeds keep rotting instead of growing. Explain what is going wrong and suggest a solution.
realProblems.answer: She should use suckers (asexual reproduction), because the new plants are exact copies of the parent and will give the same sweet fruit. Seeds would mix features and might give different fruit.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She should use suckers (asexual reproduction), because the new plants are exact copies of the parent and will give the same sweet fruit. Seeds would mix features and might give different fruit.

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants every new banana plant to give the same sweet fruit as her best plant. Should she use seeds or suckers? Explain your choice.
realProblems.answer: They should choose sorghum, because it is drought-resistant and can grow with little water, whereas maize needs more water and might fail in a dry year.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: They should choose sorghum, because it is drought-resistant and can grow with little water, whereas maize needs more water and might fail in a dry year.

realProblems.prompt: In a very dry year, a family must choose between planting maize or sorghum. Which should they choose and why?
realProblems.answer: The seedling bends toward the glass because it is growing toward the light it needs for photosynthesis. This response to light helps the plant get as much sunlight as possible.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The seedling bends toward the glass because it is growing toward the light it needs for photosynthesis. This response to light helps the plant get as much sunlight as possible.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf's seedling on the windowsill is bending toward the glass. Explain why this is happening.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three main parts of a seed.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The seed coat, the food store (cotyledons), and the embryo. These are the three parts of every seed.
Germination is the process where a resting seed begins to grow into a new plant. It starts when the seed takes in water.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the three conditions a seed needs to germinate.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Water, warmth, and air. These three conditions must be present for a seed to germinate.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a seed does not need light to germinate.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A seed does not need light because it feeds on its own food store while underground. Light is only needed later, when the leaves reach the surface and start photosynthesis.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the food store inside a seed shrink as the plant grows?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The food store shrinks because the growing embryo uses it up for energy until the plant can make its own food with green leaves in the light.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two things, apart from water, that a growing plant needs to stay healthy.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any two of: light, nutrients, space, suitable temperature. A growing plant needs these along with water to stay healthy.

questions.question: Which part of a seed is the tiny baby plant?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: embryo The embryo is the tiny baby plant; the other parts protect or feed it.

questions.question: What is the main job of the seed coat?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: to protect the inside The tough seed coat guards the embryo and food store.

questions.question: Which of these does a seed NOT need to germinate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: light Seeds use their own food store, so they germinate even in the dark. Light matters only after leaves appear.

questions.question: During germination, which part usually appears first?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the root The root (radicle) grows down first to anchor the plant and take in water.

questions.question: A seed that is alive but resting and not growing is said to be:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dormant A dormant seed is alive but resting, waiting for the right conditions.

questions.question: Green plants make their own food by a process called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: photosynthesis Photosynthesis uses sunlight to make sugar (food) in the leaves.

questions.question: Which of these is a drought-resistant crop grown in dry areas of East Africa?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sorghum Sorghum survives with little water, so it suits dry East African areas.

questions.question: A new banana plant is usually grown from:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can name and describe the three main parts of a seed: the embryo, the food store, and the seed coat.
I can explain germination and put its stages in the correct order, from the dry seed to the first green leaves.
I can list the conditions a seed needs to germinate (water, warmth, and air) and explain why light is NOT needed for germination.
I can describe what a growing plant needs to stay healthy: light, water, nutrients from the soil, space, and a suitable temperature.
I can plan and carry out a fair test about plant growth, changing only one thing at a time and keeping everything else the same.
I can measure and record plant growth carefully using a ruler and a results table, and draw a simple growth graph.
I can describe the life cycle of a flowering plant: seed, germination, growth, flower, fruit, and new seeds.
I can explain asexual reproduction and give local examples: banana suckers, sweet-potato and cassava cuttings, and onion bulbs.
